sentences.text: My name is Norbert Friedman, N-O-R-B-E-R-T F-R-I-E-D-M-A-N.
That was my name at birth.
I had a nickname in-- in Poland.
Practically everyone had a nickname that ended with E-K, so my name was Nolek.
N-O-L-E-K.
December 20, 1922.
In Krakow, Poland.
K-R-A-K-O-W.
As I said, I was born December 20, 1922.
I was born to a observant Jewish family.
I was the first born.
My father was a kosher butcher, one of 12 children.
His father was also a kosher butcher, so were two of his older brothers.
My father was born in the small town of [?
Vialopolis ?] [?
Krinske. ?] Later on, his parents moved out to a small community of [?
Zaubuoche, ?] which was adjacent to the town of Zywiec.
Jews were not allowed to live in Zywiec because the Archprince of Zywiec prohibited Jews from living there.
So although they worked in-- in the town, they lived in the adjacent small town.
My mother was born in the town of Krakow.
She was one of six children.
She had three sisters and two brothers.

My grandmother-- my grandmother's-- my grandmother's house was actually the house in which I grew up because, although we moved out to a different address, I spent most of my time and my childhood up till the age of eight in her house.

And she was probably the dominant influence in my thinking and my set of values and my ethical and moral standards which she tried to explain to me in common terms, but were based mostly on Jewish ethical teachings.
My maternal grandfather came from a small town of [INAUDIBLE].
He was an Orthodox Jew, and he was a wholesale fish dealer.
My grandmother was a descendant of a very well known rabbinical family.
Her maiden name was [?
Bochner. ?] She came from the town of Chrzanow.
Her grandfather was a Chrzanow [?
Hasidic, ?] which means the-- the pious or the [? wunderrabbi ?] of Chrzanow.
Despite the fact that she was very observant and very knowledgeable and gave the children a Jewish education, she also gave all her children a secular education.
My mother, besides going through religious school of [?
Batheokow, ?] also attended the middle school, common school.
My mother, besides speaking Hebrew, Yiddish, and Polish, studied other languages.
She spoke French.
She read in those languages, French, German, and Esperanto.
My father, as I said, was a butcher.
And the best I remember up to till the age of six, he had a small butcher shop.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Later on, in 1928, 1929, when the Depression came to Poland, he had to abandon the butcher shop, and he was helping out my grandmother and my grandfather and his wholesale fish business.
We-- my mother was a romantic, loved to read books, preferred the bucolic settings for her-- for the house.
And she preferred to live outside what was known as the ghetto, so we always wound up living sometime in the suburbs where there were trees and-- and, you know, pastoral settings.
But I had to commute to school.
I went to the [NON-ENGLISH] at the age of four and five.
And later on, I went to-- to a Hebrew public school.
That particular school was an annex to the Hebrew high school, which was an accredited high school.
And the-- the language that was taught, basically, now in the school was Polish.
However, the second language was Hebrew.
And the Hebrew history and Bible was subject.
It was not an Orthodox school.
I would-- I would call it a-- today probably compared to modern-- to modern Orthodox, because all the laws of kashrut and holidays were observed.
And-- and boys were required to wear hats, and prayers were said before the school day started, Hebrew prayers.
As I was growing up, I was exposed to a very warm, loving family life.
My grandmother's house was the center, the hub, of the-- all family activities.
On Saturday afternoon, her children with the grandchildren would come to the house, and family affairs would be discussed.
My grandmother would tell the children what-- what problems would arise in a family.
If somebody had financial problems, you know, she would seek the help of other-- of her other children.
If relatives came from out of town from small villages to visit or came to go to [INAUDIBLE], she made sure that they were put up.
She was very involved in-- in charity.
She was like the chairlady for a charity organization called [?
Gimelat ?] [?

Hassid. ?] I remember every Wednesday night, she was out on town, going to her friends, collecting money either for a dowry for a girl who did not have the money or for a widow who was desolate or for orphans.
We did not have welfare organizations.
It was-- the welfare was provided by the community itself, by people in the community.
I remember my mother and my aunt getting together on the evenings playing the mandolin, singing the popular songs.
And I remember playing with my cousins, the children of my mother's sisters.
It-- it was a life that the memories of it return to me occasionally in my dreams.
Most likely I do not remember the harshness of economic difficulties, but I remember the love and the warmth and the care and the protective umbrella that the family provided for the children.
The town that we lived in, Krakow, was a beautiful medieval town with castles and winding streets, with parks.
And the Vistula River ran around the city, almost embracing it.
I did not know many non-Jewish children because I went to the Hebrew school, and we lived in the-- in-- my grandmother lived in a strictly Jewish quarters, what was known as the Kazimierz.
The only non-Jewish children I knew was from the neighborhood where we lived.
But as I said, I commuted by streetcar to the school, and I usually stayed with my grandmother for holidays.
I-- I fondly remember the Friday evenings where I used to go with my grandfather to his small house of prayer called the shtibl, which is a small house.
And I remember accompanying him to services and-- and listening to the discussions.
And he was-- he was revered for his knowledge of Talmud.
And I used to bask in the glory of his, you know, of-- of his prominence.
My-- my grandfather passed away when I was six years old.
And as I said, my-- my father started to help out my grandmother and her business.
In 1930, I think, when I was eight, my grandmother herself passed away.
I remember it distinctly, even, you know, so many years ago, she-- she was laying sick in her bed.
And she was knitting a woolen outfit.
I remember the color of the outfit.
It was a coral pink.
She was knitting the scarf and the-- and the earmuffs and gloves.
And when she finished, she just folded her their hands and the needles, and she expired.
It was-- was-- [INAUDIBLE] was passing.
The business that my grandfather was in, you know, also ceased to exist.

And because of the very, very difficult times that prevailed in those days, my father started seeking employment and went out to the town of Bielsko which was about 60 miles, 96 kilometers, from Krakow.
It was a town close to the Czech and German border.
It was long before the [?
Silesia. ?] And found employment in a large butcher shop there.
After a few months, he brought my mother and myself to the city of Bielsko, and we found an apartment in an adjacent small town suburb which was called Biala.
It was in a different-- it was the district of Krakow.
I enrolled in a Jewish day school.
And I spent the following four years in the school, the fourth, fifth, sixth, and seventh grade.
The school only had seven grades.

And after a short amount of time, my father enrolled me in a [NON-ENGLISH] because he felt that the Jewish education at that particular school was not-- you know, would not complete my Jewish, you know, knowledge.
But because of the fact that I knew less than other boys of my age, I was put in the class of boys about two years younger than I was, and I just didn't feel comfortable.
And I started skipping class.
So after a certain amount of time, my father went to pay the tuition, and the rabbi told him, why are you paying?
Your son is not attending.
So my father was perplexed because he used to see me leave home in the afternoon, supposedly going to the [NON-ENGLISH].
And he followed me, and he saw me playing soccer with other boys.
And I played goalie, and I made a good catch.
And suddenly I heard applause, and I turned around.
There was my father standing in the corner.
Well, I-- there was a price to be paid.
I didn't sit very comfortably for the next couple weeks.
But my father realized I-- I was not eager to pursue the [? ultra ?] religious education and left me in the-- in that school.
Bielsko was a beautiful town.
It was a town nestled at the-- at the-- at the feet off the Beskidi Mountains.
The-- it was bilingual, German and Polish, because before the war-- before World War I ended, it was under German occupation.
And this is why I acquired my knowledge of-- of German.
It was cultural.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Jewish community was closely knit.
It was a textile town.
They manufactured woolens that were considered second only to the Manchester woolens in England.
Most of the textile factories were owned by Jewish people, the families [?
Rabinowitz, ?] Rappaport, and Wolfe and Frankel, I think, were main owners.
We had dye houses and-- knitting-- and mills where-- where the material was being manufactured.
Most of my-- my friends in-- in-- in the Hebrew school, in the Jewish school, were children of-- of middle class Jewish families.
Some of them were affluent, some less affluent.
The-- the boys and girls attended school separately till the seventh grade.
In the seventh grade, it became a-- coeducational.
After seventh year-- after the seventh grade, after I graduated from the school, I-- I was only 13 years old, and was felt that I should attend-- still attend some school.
And I went to the-- to a Polish public school for-- for the eighth grade.
In the interim, I-- I enrolled in a Zionist organization.
And I didn't enroll for the ideology.
I enrolled because they had the best soccer field from the other organizations.
And we played over there, so this was the attraction.
Later on, I was exposed to the teaching of this particular-- this was a leftist organization, [INAUDIBLE].
And I was exposed to-- to the teachings of-- of materialism and social structures.
My-- my idea, my plan was to go to Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And many things happened that enforced my desire to go to Israel.
In 1935, our town suffered a pogrom.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A Jewish innkeeper in a quarrel with one of his customers, accidentally shot the customer.
And what happened afterwards is difficult to describe because for over a week, all Jewish homes were closed.
Jews wouldn't go out on the street.

The non-Jewish homes displayed pictures of the Holy Mother and crosses and candles so that people who came out as far as 30 miles away on foot to rob, plunder, and-- and kill would know which is a Jewish home, which is not a Jewish home.
Jewish businesses were destroyed.
You couldn't get out of town even if you had a lot of money to pay for-- for-- for transportation to get out.
You couldn't get out of town.
Police did not intervene.
The gave them free hand.
We were fortunate.
As I said, my mother had a inclination to live in the suburbs.
We lived in the non-Jewish section.
We were the first Jews that moved into this particular section.
After we moved in, we were treated the way some black families are treated when they move-- when they're the first families that move in the white neighborhood.

I spent two weeks in bed recovering from the beating that I received from-- from the children in the neighborhood and who later became my friends, with whom I later played soccer, and who were the ones who stood guard at the entrance of the street so that the mob would not come and plunder.
But the-- the-- as I was growing up, the anti-Semitic trend in Poland became more and more apparent to me.
In 1935, when Marshal Pilsudski passed away-- Marshal Pilsudski was the marshal, wasn't the dictator, but he was the head of the Polish government.
And he tried to make sure that the Jews were afforded some protection.
But with his-- with his passing, many of the laws that they tried to institute became-- you know, were put on books.
One of the laws was the law of ritual slaughter.
They tried to abolish ritual slaughter altogether.
But they finally compromised, and they allowed about one third of the amount of animals that were slaughtered for kosher meat to be slaughtered.
And things became very tough.
My father lost his job, and the butcher shop that he opened afterwards wouldn't function.
There were many economic hardships that were-- that were upon the Jews.
For instance, just to give an example, we had only one savings and loan association in Poland called the PKO, which was Pocztowa Kasa Oszczednosci, which is the Polish savings and loan association.
It was affiliated with the post office.
They had over 10,000 offices through Poland. 60% of the money that was deposited in there was Jewish deposits.
There wasn't one Jew that was allowed to work for that-- for that company.
For Jews to-- to pursue a trade was made very difficult.
You-- you know, in Poland, probably as other places in Europe, they used to have market days.
So the Jews were not allowed to purchase anything till about 10 o'clock till the Poles, the non-Jews, had an opportunity to buy whatever they wanted.
After that-- you know, after they satisfied their needs, that's when the Jews were allowed to make purchases.
Before-- before 1918, many of the tobacco kiosks were-- were in the hand of Jewish merchants.
That was taken away.
Anything that was run by the government, which was controlled by the government, such as-- it was a government cartel-- such as sugar, alcohol, matches, salt.
Any of these items were not allowed to be traded by Jews.
OK?
Even-- I mean, in the wholesale trading, they were-- they were restricted from-- from doing their business.
They were restricted from-- from export and import business, although some did.
But it took a great deal of ingenuity and great deal of bribery.
And, you know, there were famous stories that circulate about the Jew is making the bill of lading.
He's shipping pigs to-- to-- to Germany.
And he's making out the bill of lading.
He puts down Polish grapes.
And the other man asks him, why are you putting grapes?
You're shipping pigs.
He says, you don't want me to put down Polish pigs and go to a camp, and go to jail.
Because that-- that-- that's what it was.

I remember walking on the street with my mother, a Friday afternoon, and being jostled by a Polish-- drunken Polish soldier and then accused for having called him a Polish pig and being taken and arrested and put in jail.
And only due to the fact that my father knew the Polish Commissar for the police, we were released before the Sabbath.
And all these incidents contributed, you know, to my conviction that there was no future for-- for a Jew in Poland.
All this became much more apparent to me in 1936 when I finished the eighth grade, and I applied to-- to a polytechnic in our town.
It was a very well-known polytechnical school because it offered courses in-- in textiles and dyeing and also a mechanical department.
And I wanted to go to the mechanical department because I felt that was the only trade that would help me-- that I would be able to contribute something was-- when I get to Israel.
And we went through two days of written exams.
And then we were supposed to go to-- for two days of oral exams.
But because of the excellency of my written exams, my oral exams were waived.

But when the day came when they announced, you know, who was-- whoa was admitted, my name was called, and I heard the words 'passed, not admitted.' The fact was that only one Jew was admitted to-- to-- to the class.
They practiced in Poland what was known as numerus clausus.
In-- in schools of-- in middle schools and institutions of higher learning and universities, that meant that only a certain amount of Jews were admitted.
And, of course, this made very difficult for Jews to-- to-- to acquire an education in Poland.
And many of them used to leave Poland for their higher education.
They had-- and it was very well known that the universities of Poland, they had what would be known over here as a Jim Crow line, that the Jews had to sit only in the certain spots.
And as a protest, Jewish students never sat down.
They used to stand in the back of a class.
Through the-- through the four years of their study at the university, Jewish students would never sit down.
They would stand for the four years as a protest, and besides the fact that there were incidents of anti-Semitism and beating by the academia and the exclusion from professions.
In 1938, if I remember correctly, the Polish government issued a-- a law where they barred Jews-- Jewish attorneys from the Bar Association.
They wouldn't admit any more Jews.
They were complaining about the fact that ratio-wise, it-- it was out of proportion.
The amount of-- of Jews in the profession were not in proportion to-- to the amount of Jewish people.
Jews constituted 10% of the Polish population.
Out of 35 million Poles, about three and a half million were Jews.
We were the second largest minority.
The largest minorities were the Ukrainians.
The second largest minority were the Jews.
The Ukrainians were not as persecuted as Jews because they were a force, and they were all concentrated in one part of the country, in the-- in the southeastern part of Poland.
But the Jews as a minority were-- were officially and unofficially persecuted.
There-- there were organizations, especially academic organizations, the [?
ED, ?] that-- that seeked exclusion of Jews from the country.
They looked to-- to have the Jews forcefully sent out from the country.

And as a matter of fact, it became now public domain that-- that contact between Polish authorities and President Roosevelt, as far as President Roosevelt helping the Polish government to send the Jews out to-- to-- I think Somalia and Madagascar were the places that were considered.
And President Roosevelt's suggestion was to take the young Jews first and send them out, because this way you would open up a labor market for the-- for the-- for the young Poles.
Despite all of this, despite all of this, the life that we led [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a life of comradeship among the Jewish people.

My-- my-- my fondest memory of my friends with whom I belonged to the organizations, the-- the weekends which we used to spend going skiing into the mountains or going hiking into the mountains and going on summer camps.
The last summer camp that I attended was in 1939 on the Romanian border.
It was a leadership summer camp for my Zionist organization.
I was scheduled to go to Israel in the winter of 1939.
And we were going-- we were scheduled to go on the illegal [NON-ENGLISH] because of the white paper, the Balfour Declaration, that limited the amount of immigration to Israel.
When I was in Israel several years ago, I spoke to a friend of mine who spent that summer with me at the camp.
And he brought to my attention that the leader of the uprising in the Warsaw ghetto, Anielewicz, was with us at that particularly camp.
And the only thing that he remembered about him was that he was a very good soccer player.
That's all we remember.
We had no idea that he would attain such a position in history of the Jewish people.
All--
When we came to Bielsko, and my-- my-- the job that my father had offered probably a better financial situation for us.
We had a nice apartment.
And when I was 10 years old, my brother was born.
My brother's name was Oscar.
We-- we had to engage a girl to live with us to-- to care for him because my mother would help my father in his business.
And later on, he got bigger, I used to help take care of him.
I used to take him to the kindergarten, and I would pick him up after school.
We loved each other very much, despite the difference in ages.
There was 10 years of difference.
And there was a-- there was a very-- apparently, he-- he as a younger brother idolized me.
And I-- I was very protective about him and tried to teach him all the things that I knew about my young life.

In 1939-- in the summer of 1939, shortly before the outbreak of the war, he went to my parental grandparents to live with my grandmother because one of-- one of my father's brothers died of a heart attack, and my grandmother was very heartbroken.
By the way, my grandmother was not-- was my father's aunt, because when my father's brother was born, the second brother, his mother passed away.
And as was the custom in Poland, my grandfather married her sister.
So the-- the 10 consecutive children afterwards were three quarter brothers, because they were-- they were cousins and half-brothers to my father.
But she-- she loved-- she loved my brother very much.
Now that I mention the home of my parental-- my paternal grandparents, a story comes to my mind.
When I was six years old--
